coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of surface protein-imprinted nanoparticles based on metal coordination and anchored carbon dots for enhanced fluorescence detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhiqiang
Zhang, Shiting
Jin, Siyu
Feng, Xingjia
Bai, Yufei
Han, Xiao
Fu, Guoqi
coredata.dc:description: Molecularly imprinted polymers endowed with photo-luminescent properties have attracted wide research interest in many fields such as biological analysis and diseases diagnosis. Herein, we illustrate a versatile method for the construction of surface protein-imprinted nanoparticles based on metal coordination and anchored carbon dots (CDs) for enhanced fluorescence detection of the target protein. As the fluorescent nanosupports for surface imprinting, CDs-attached SiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized via thiol–ene click chemistry. With histidine (His)-exposed protein as templates, imprinted nanoshells were formed over the nanosupports via copolymerization of a Cu2+-chelating monomer and an oligo (ethylene glycol) monomer, hence producing high-quality imprinted cavities because of both the relatively strong coordination and inhibited non-specific binding. Using lysozyme as a model His-exposed template, the imprinted nanoparticles showed fluorescence enhancement while binding the target protein, and exhibited significantly increased specific fluorescence response than the controls without the metal coordination. They achieved a high imprinting factor of 5.8 and a low limit of detection of 10.1 nM. Furthermore, such sensors were applied to determine lysozyme in diluted chicken egg-white samples with satisfactory recoveries at three spiking levels ranging from 97.9 to 101.4%. Human serum albumin was also used as another template protein for preliminary confirming the generality of the presented strategy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Protein imprinting
Surface imprinting
Metal coordination
Carbon dots
Thiol–ene click chemistry
Fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of vinyl functionalized and CDs anchored SiO2 NPs
Synthesis and characterization of FL surface Lyz-imprinted NPs
Optimized synthesis of the MI-FL sensors
FL sensing performance of the MIP NPs
Preliminary extension to imprinting of HSA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21674051 ) and the National Key R&D Program of China ( 2017YFC11044001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) 1H NMR spectra of the CDs and GMA-CDs samples dissolved in D2O. (B) UV–vis spectra of (a) SiO2-CDs and (b) SiO2-SH NPs dispersed in phosphate buffer (pH 8.0, 50 mM) containing Ellman's reagent, together with the corresponding images under a 365 nm UV lamp. The inset plot shows the calibration curve for quantitating thiol groups using a cysteine standard at λ = 412 nm. (C) Zeta potentials of (a) CDs, (b) GMA-CDs, (c) SiO2, (d) SiO2-SH, (e) SiO2-CDs, (f) SiO2-CDs-MPS, (g) Lyz-MIP and (h) NIP NPs in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 10 mM). (D) Transmittance of Lyz-MIP NPs (1 mg/mL) in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 10 mM) with increasing temperatures (λ = 660 nm). The insets show the images of the dispersions after 1-h setting at (a) 4 °C and (b) 37 °C, respectively.

(A) The FL emission spectra of GMA-CDs (a), SiO2-CDs-MPS (b) and Lyz-MIP NPs (c) in phosphate buffer. The nanoparticles' concentrations: GMA-CDs, 0.3 μg/mL; SiO2-CDs-MPS and MIP, 200 μg/mL. The insets presents the images of the MIP dispersions under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right), respectively. (B) The absorption spectrum of Lyz (0.6 mg/mL) (a), and FL emission spectra of the Lyz-MIP NPs in the absence of Lyz (b) and in the presence of Lyz (1.6 μM) (c). (C) FL response kinetics of the MIP and NIP NPs to Lyz (1.6 μM). (D) The effect of temperature on the FL enhancement of the MIP and NIP NPs to Lyz (1.6 μM).

(A) The FL response of the MIP and NIP NPs synthesized with varied monomer concentrations. (B) The FL response of the MIP and NIP NPs synthesized in different manners: (a) synthesized without GMA-IDA, (b) synthesized with GMA-IDA, (c) synthesized with GMA-IDA-Cu2+ but without Cu2+ loaded after template removal, and (d) synthesized with GMA-IDA-Cu2+ and with Cu2+ loaded after template removal. C Lyz = 1.6 μM.

The FL emission spectra of (a) the MIP and (b) NIP NPs in the presence of Lyz with increasing concentrations (0–1.6 μM), respectively. The insets show the corresponding Stern–Volmer plots with the estimated K SV as well as R 2. (c) FL enhancement of the MIP and NIP NPs in the presence of different proteins (1.6 μM). The (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP values are indicated above the bars. (d) FL response of the MIP nanoparticle to Lyz (1.6 μM) mixed with Cyt c of multifold higher concentrations.

Schematic presentation of the synthesis of FL protein-imprinted NPs by aqueous precipitation copolymerization of a Cu2+ chelating monomer and an olig (ethylene glycol) monomer over CDs-attached SiO2 NPs.
Spiked recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs, %; n = 3) for the detection of Lyz in the 500-fold diluted chicken egg-white using the Lyz-MIP NPs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The recoveries were calculated based on the Lyz concentration found in the spiked samples after subtraction of that found in the non-spiked sample (0.48 μM).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of surface protein-imprinted nanoparticles based on metal coordination and anchored carbon dots for enhanced fluorescence detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Polymer Materials of Ministry of Education, Institute of Polymer Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin, 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Polymer Materials of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Polymer Materials of Ministry of Education, Institute of Polymer Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin, 300071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecularly imprinted polymers endowed with photo-luminescent properties have attracted wide research interest in many fields such as biological analysis and diseases diagnosis. Herein, we illustrate a versatile method for the construction of surface protein-imprinted nanoparticles based on metal coordination and anchored carbon dots (CDs) for enhanced fluorescence detection of the target protein. As the fluorescent nanosupports for surface imprinting, CDs-attached SiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized via thiol–ene click chemistry. With histidine (His)-exposed protein as templates, imprinted nanoshells were formed over the nanosupports via copolymerization of a Cu2+-chelating monomer and an oligo (ethylene glycol) monomer, hence producing high-quality imprinted cavities because of both the relatively strong coordination and inhibited non-specific binding. Using lysozyme as a model His-exposed template, the imprinted nanoparticles showed fluorescence enhancement while binding the target protein, and exhibited significantly increased specific fluorescence response than the controls without the metal coordination. They achieved a high imprinting factor of 5.8 and a low limit of detection of 10.1 nM. Furthermore, such sensors were applied to determine lysozyme in diluted chicken egg-white samples with satisfactory recoveries at three spiking levels ranging from 97.9 to 101.4%. Human serum albumin was also used as another template protein for preliminary confirming the generality of the presented strategy.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are artificially synthesized polymer receptors, which outperform the natural receptors due to their high stability, ease of obtention and low cost. The synthesis of MIPs usually involves copolymerization of functional and crosslinking monomers around template molecules. After template removal, specific imprinted cavities are generated in the resultant MIP matrix. MIPs have been widely used in a variety of fields, such as affinity separation media, molecular recognition elements for sensors, and specific target-capturing materials in ligand binding assays [1]. The imprinting of proteins and other biomacromolecules is still challenged in comparison with that against small molecules because of their large size, complex structure and variable conformation [2–4]. Therefore, a variety of approaches have been developed, such as surface imprinting [5–11], nanoscale imprinting [12–14], epitope imprinting [15], and imprinting based on hydrogel films [16]. Among these, surface imprinting over diverse nanomaterials, such as SiO2 or Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs), carbon nanotubes and quantum dots (QDs), has received much attention due to the advantages such as ease macromolecular diffusion, high binding capacity, and facile integration of the functions of the encapsulated nanocores [17].

As an important application of protein MIPs, recent years have witnessed a large number of studies on sensitive protein detection by combining the selective recognition features of the MIPs with the sensing properties of photo-luminescent materials [4,18,19]. The fluorescence (FL) materials used for the construction of the molecular imprinting-based FL sensors (MI-FL sensors) include inorganic semiconductor QDs (e.g. CdTe, CdS and Mn–ZnS QDs) [20–27], carbon-based QDs consisting of carbon quantum dots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [28–33] and lanthanide-doped upconversion NPs (UCNPs) [34]. Among them, carbon-based QDs have recently attracted more attention, because of their superiority in terms of facile synthesis as well as modification, robust chemical inertness, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, in comparison to traditional semiconductor QDs and organic dyes [35].

The sensing performance of MI-FL sensors is usually characterized by the limit of detection (LOD), linear detection range and spike recovery toward a target protein, and imprinting factor (IF) which is based on the FL response ratio of the MIPs with respect to the corresponding non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) on protein binding [18]. For increasing these sensing properties, one kind of methods is aimed at the FL substances, such as construction of ratiometric FL detection system with two FL materials [27,33] and post-imprinting modification of the pre-formed molecularly imprinted cavities with FL reporters [36]. These methods usually involve rather complicated synthesis procedures. On the other hand, any measures beneficial to enhancing the specific recognition features of MIPs can be adopted to improve the optsensing properties of FL MIPs. In this context, our previous study proved that the FL sensing performance of the CdTe QDs-based protein-imprinted NPs could be enhanced by reducing non-specific binding through post-imprinting PEGylation [37]. Based on strengthening of the interactions with protein templates, a variety of FL glycoprotein-imprinted sensors were fabricated by use of vinylized or surface immobilized phenylboronic acids, which can form reversible covalent interactions with the cis-diol-containing proteins [24,30,38,39]. Tan et al. [40] synthesized highly efficient QDs-based MI-FL sensors for protein detection using thiol-terminated aptamers as a functional monomer, which can bind to the target in a specific manner analogous to antibody–antigen interactions; however, it is not an easy task for screening and synthesizing of aptamers toward specific templates.

It has been well-confirmed in quite a few of previous studies [41–46] that Cu2+ or Ni2+ mediated coordination can significantly enhance protein imprinting. We demonstrated fabrication of thermo-responsive surface protein-imprinted NPs using both a Cu2+-chelating monomer and an oligo (ethylene glycol) (OEG) monomer via aqueous precipitation polymerization [45]. The as-synthesized protein-imprinted NPs showed remarkable binding selectivity and affinity, which can be attributed to both the rather strong metal coordination with histidine (His)-exposed protein molecules and the protein-resistant feature of the non-linear PEG dominated MIP matrix. To the best of our knowledge, however, such metal coordination has not been utilized for fabrication of MI-FL sensors, probably due to that semiconductor QDs like CdTe QDs principally used in the early MI-FL sensor studies can be permanently quenched by transition metal ions.

Herein, we demonstrate a novel approach for the construction of MI-FL sensors based on surface protein imprinting over CDs-anchored SiO2 NPs by utilization of metal coordination. CDs were covalently anchored on the surface of SiO2 NPs via click chemistry in order to make the FL reporters approach the imprinted cavities as close as possible. The above described pre-polymerization system consisting of both a metal chelating monomer and an OEG monomer [45] was adapted for the formation of protein-imprinted nanoshells. As a proof of concept, we firstly chose lysozyme (Lyz) as the model His-exposed protein template to investigate the synthesis, characterization and sensing performance of the resultant MI-FL sensors, and then preliminarily examined the generality of this method by using human serum albumin (HSA) as another template protein.
See supporting information.

Vinyl functionalized CDs were synthesized according to the literature method [47]. Briefly, the CDs with amine groups on their surface were synthesized by a simple microwave-assisted pyrolysis process using acrylic acid and ethylenediamine as the carbon source and surface passivation agent, respectively. Glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) was allowed to react with the amine groups on the CDs’ surface, resulting in GMA modified CDs (denoted as GMA-CDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of vinyl functionalized and CDs anchored SiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The procedures were detailed in supporting information. Briefly, SiO2 NPs were synthesized by the Stöber method [48] and then were modified with 3-mercaptopropyltrimethoxysilane to obtain thiol-functionalized SiO2 NPs (denoted as SiO2-SH). The GMA-CDs were anchored on the surface of the SiO2-SH NPs via by aqueous thiol-Michael addition click reaction using tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine hydrochloride as a catalyst, and the resultant nanocomposites were named as SiO2-CDs. The SiO2-CDs nanocomposites were further coated with a thin SiO2 layer and subsequently modified with of 3-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (MPS) via a one-pot sol-gel method, thus affording SiO2-CDs-MPS NPs.

The surface Lyz-imprinted NPs were synthesized using the SiO2-CDs-MPS NPs as the nanosupports according to our previous study [45] with some modification. GMA-iminodiacetic acid (IDA)-Cu2+ solution was prepared by mixing the GMA-IDA solution with equimolar CuSO4 (0.1 M). Lyz (18 mg) was allowed to coordinate with a two-fold molar excess of GMA-IDA-Cu2+ for 1 h in 2 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 10 mM). This solution was then added to 18 mL of the phosphate buffer containing SiO2-CDs-MPS (200 mg), di(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate (MEO2MA, 56.0 mg), N,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, 2.6 mg) and acrylamide (AAm, 1.4 mg) with a total monomer concentration of 0.3% and the molar ratio of MEO2MA/MBA/AAm to be 0.89/0.05/0.06. Then, pre-assembly, radical-initiated polymerization, template removal and Cu2+ reloading were conducted sequentially in the same way as described in the previous work [45]. Briefly, the polymerization was performed under magnetic stirring at 37 °C for 12 h after injecting 200 μL of sodium dodecylsulfate (SDS) solution (2%, w/v), 300 μL of APS solution (2.5%, w/v) together with 150 μL of TEMED solution (1.25%, v/v) to the dispersion. Afterwards, the resultant imprinted NPs were collected by centrifugation, washed at 4 °C sequentially with water to remove unreacted monomers, with EDTA-2Na+ solution (0.1 M) to desorb Cu2+, and then with 1% SDS/1% acetic acid solution to remove the embedded protein template. NaCl solution (1 M) was used to remove the remaining SDS and acetic acid, and then washed with water to remove remaining NaCl. The NPs were then loaded with Cu2+ by incubation in CuSO4 solution (0.1 M) for 30 min, washed extensively with distillated water, and finally freeze-dried for further use. The control non-imprinted particles were prepared in the same way but without the addition of the template. The final imprinted and non-imprinted particles are denoted as MIP and NIP, respectively.

FL spectra were recorded using a fluorospectrophotometer (F-2710, Hitachi, Japan), with the silt widths of excitation and emission set at 5 nm and 10 nm, respectively, and the photomultiplier tube voltage set at 700 V. Unless stated otherwise, all the NPs for FL measurements were dispersed in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 10 mM) with a concentration of 200 μg/mL. UV–Vis absorption was measured on a spectrophotometer (TU-1900, Purkinje General Instrument Co., China) equipped with a temperature controller. The morphologies and structures of the NPs were observed by a TEM (Talos F200X G2, FEI, Czech) and a SEM (Apreo S LoVac, FEI, Czech). Zeta potentials of the NPs were measured by DLS (Zeta Sizer Nano-ZS90, Malvern, UK). Surface elemental compositions were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos Analytical Ltd, UK). TGA was performed by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TG 209, NETZSCH, Germany) under nitrogen atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C/min up to 800 °C.

Chicken egg-white containing Lyz was chosen as a real biological matrix to examine the practicability and accuracy of the Lyz-imprinted NPs for FL detection of the target protein. Briefly, chicken egg-white was diluted 500-fold with phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 10 mM) and the supernatant was obtained after centrifugation. The imprinted NPs (0.2 mg) were added into the supernatant solution (1 mL) spiked with Lyz at three concentration levels. The FL spectra of the dispersion were recorded and all the tests were performed in triplicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of FL surface Lyz-imprinted NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of FL surface protein-imprinted NPs is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The vinyl modified CDs (GMA-CDs) were facilely prepared according to the literature method [47], consisting of microwave-assisted pyrolysis and subsequent aqueous functionalization with GMA. Cu2+-chelating monomer (GMA-IDA-Cu2+) was obtained by aqueous one-pot reaction of IDA disodium salt with equimolar GMA, followed by chelating with equimolar Cu2+ under neutral condition. A large number of GMA-CDs were anchored on thiol-modified SiO2 NPs by thiol–ene click reaction [49] and the resultant SiO2-CDs core–satellite nanocomposites were then further encapsulated with a thin SiO2 shell bearing vinyl groups by a one-pot sol-gel process. Such well-defined core–satellites–shell nanostructures (SiO2-CDs-MPS) would make the FL reporters fully approach the imprinted cavities generated after subsequent surface imprinting and hence favor sensitive FL detection [21]. This is different from the SiO2 NPs encapsulating random CDs previously employed for the synthesis of CDs based MI-FL sensors [29,31]. Surface imprinting of His-exposed proteins over the SiO2-CDs-MPS NPs was performed via aqueous precipitation polymerization according to our previous work [45]. GMA-IDA-Cu2+ was used as the metal-chelating monomer for coordination with the imidazole groups of the exposed His residues, AAm as the assistant functional monomers for hydrogen bonding with the protein template, and an OEG monomer (MEO2MA) as the main monomer which made the imprinted coatings both thermos-responsive and PEGylation-like.

As observed by TEM, the size of the CDs was about 2 nm (supporting information, Fig. S1a). Compared with the CDs, the GMA-CDs showed both the excitation and emission spectra with little red shift (supporting information, Fig. S2). Furthermore, the 1H NMR peaks at δ 6.00 and δ 5.59 (–CCH2) seen in the spectrum of the GMA-CDs (Fig. 1 A) proved the successful surface modification of CDs with GMA. The SiO2 NPs were prepared via Stöber method with an average diameter of ∼145 nm (supporting information, Fig. S1b), and then were surface modified with thiol groups for immobilizing GMA-CDs via thiol–ene click reaction. The initial thiol amount of the SiO2-SH NPs and the residual amount of the SiO2-CDs NPs were measured by the Ellman's method [50] (Fig. 2 B). The thiol content of the SiO2-CDs NPs was estimated to be 12.5 μmol/g, decreased to about 30% that of the SiO2-SH NPs. Moreover, the SiO2-CDs NPs dispersed in water showed bright blue FL under a 365 nm UV lamp, in contrast to the SiO2-SH NPs showing no FL. These results indicated successful attachment of GMA-CDs to the SiO2-SH NPs. The change in the zeta potentials of the different NPs can also support the success of the each step of surface functionalization (Fig. 2C).

With Lyz as a model protein template bearing one exposed His residue, protein-imprinted NPs were synthesized by polymerization over the SiO2-CDs-MPS NPs. The morphologies of the resultant MIP and NIP NPs together with the nanosupports were observed by TEM and SEM, respectively. As shown from the TEM images (supporting information, Figs. S1c–e), the MIP and NIP NPs were significantly rough than the SiO2-CDs-MPS NPs, suggesting the existing of imprinted and no-imprinted layers, although these polymer layers could not be discriminated due to their small thickness in dry state. As can be seen from the SEMmicrographs (supporting information, Fig. S3), the MIP and NIP NPs showed somewhat conglutination due to the radical-induced polymerization on the surface of the SiO2-CDs-MPS NPs. The XPS analysis result also proved the success of the MIP-layer formation (supporting information, Fig. S4). The TG curves further confirmed the formation of the MIP and NIP layers over the SiO2-CDs-MPS NPs, which were estimated to 12.0 wt% and 6.8 wt% relative to the internal nanocores, respectively (supporting information, Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. 1D, the MIP nanoshells showed thermo-responsiveness, and their volume phase-transition temperature (VPTT) was estimated to be ∼26 °C, which coincided with the reported lower critical solution temperature (LCST) of linear PMEO2MA [51].

The MIP NPs showed the strongest FL emission at λ em 394 nm under λ ex 320 nm (supporting information, Fig. S6a), and hence this λ ex was chosen in the following investigation. The FL emission could remain stable within 20 min, long enough for FL measurements (supporting information, Fig. S6b). As shown from Fig. 2A, the MIP and SiO2-CDs-MPS NPs shared rather strong emission peaks at λ em of 394 nm with a much low concentration (200 μg/mL), although both the spectra became less symmetric and showed slightly blue-shift compared with that of the GMA-CDs. It is noted that, different from the previously reported CDs-based protein-imprinted nanomaterials with FL quenching on protein binding (so-called “turn-off” type) [29–31], the MIP NPs demonstrated FL enhancement in the presence of Lyz (so-called “turn-on” type) (Fig. 2B), hence beneficial for sensitive FL detection in comparison with the “turn-off” type [18]. This may be attributed to that we employed the unique GMA-CDs with plenty of amine groups synthesized via microwave-assisted pyrolysis and GMA modification. On the other hand, as seen from Fig. 2B, the FL emission spectra of the MIP NPs showed no spectral overlap with the absorption spectrum of Lyz, hence suggesting that the FL enhancement may be ascribed to the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) between the CDs and the protein [18]. With PET mechanism, Wang et al. [52] explained the “turn-on” phenomenon observed from nitrobenzoxadiazole-based 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid-imprinted FL sensors. The MIP as well as the NIP NPs exhibited a rapid FL response to Lyz binding, with the FL intensities increasing to the constant levels within about 12 min (Fig. 2C). This FL response speed is compared to that of the QDs-based surface protein-imprinted nanomaterials reported previously [25,37].

The FL enhancement is quantified by F/F0 - 1, where F0 and F are the FL intensities in the absence and presence of an analyst, respectively. The ratio of F/F0 - 1 for MIP relative to that for the corresponding NIP, i.e. relative FL enhancement denoted as (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP, can be employed to evaluate the FL response of MIP due to the specific binding to the imprinted cavities. Considering their thermos-responsiveness as shown above, the FL response of the MIP and NIP NPs to Lyz at different temperatures was measured (Fig. 2D). The MIP particles showed the highest (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP at the temperature (37 °C) they were synthesized. It is worthwhile to note that even at the temperatures below the VPTT (∼26 °C determined above) of the thermo-responsive imprinted polymer nanoshells, the MIP NPs showed little decrease in (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP. This can be explained by the template induced volume shrinking of the MIP shells, as confirmed in our previous study [53]. Despite the slightly lower (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP level at 25 °C (little below the VPTT), the following FL measurements were carried out at this temperature, since the NPs were more hydrophilic and hence illustrated better aqueous dispersion in favor of acquiring more stable FL signals. Also, pH 7.0 was chosen based on the pH effect on the FL response (supporting information, Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimized synthesis of the MI-FL sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The imprinted layer thickness of the core-shell MIP NPs may play an important role on their FL response. Therefore, the MIP and NIP NPs were synthesized with the total monomer concentrations ranging from 0.2% to 0.7% for comparing the specific FL response of the corresponding imprinted NPs. Compared with the FL nanocores (SiO2-CDs-MPS), the MIP NPs exhibited decreased FL emission (hence suggesting the increase of the imprinted layer thickness) with increasing the monomer concentration (supporting information, Fig. S8). As seen in Fig. 3 A, the MIP NPs produced with the monomer concentration of 0.3% show the highest (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP, and thus was chosen for further study.

The effect of the Cu2+ mediated coordination on the (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP was confirmed with the MIP and NIP NPs synthesized and used in the presence or absence of Cu2+ (Fig. 3B). The metal coordination based MIP NPs showed the highest (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP of 5.7, while the MIP NPs synthesized without GMA-IDA, with GMA-IDA to form electrostatic interactions with Lyz, or with GMA-IDA-Cu2+ but used without Cu2+ loaded exhibited the (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP levels of 2.7, 3.4 and 4.7, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL sensing performance of the MIP NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
For proving the ability of the Lyz-MIP NPs for sensitive detection of the target protein, the FL spectra of both the MIP and NIP NPs were recorded in the presence of Lyz with the concentrations increasing from 0 to 1.6 μM. As shown in Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b, both the MIP and NIP NPs exhibited FL enhancement response to Lyz. However, the enhancement extent for the MIP NPs were significantly larger than that for the corresponding NIP controls because of the molecular recognition sites generated in the MIP shells. The quantitative FL enhancement (F/F0 − 1), as defined above, followed the Stern−Volmer equation
(1) F/F0 − 1 = K sv C
where K SV is the Stern−Volmer constant, and C is the concentration of the target protein [21,25]. Though usually employed to describe FL quenching sensors, this equation can also be applied to FL enhancement system [54]. The ratio of K SV,MIP to K SV,NIP is defined as imprinting factor (IFSV). Within the studied range, F/F0 − 1 for both MIP and NIP NPs was well fitted to the Stern−Volmer equation, with the correlation coefficients achieving 0.99. The K sv values were estimated to be 1.199 and 0.205 μM−1 for the MIP and NIP NPs, respectively. The IFSV for the MIP was hence calculated to be 5.8. The limit of detection (LOD) of the MIP NPs was calculated following the criteria (3σ/s), where σ means the standard deviation of the blank measurements, and s means the slope of calibration curve (herein, s = K sv) [20–34]. The LOD of the MIP NPs was determined to be 10.1 nM. Compared with other MI-FL sensors for Lyz reported previously, the present sensors showed outstanding detection performance with considering both IFSV and LOD (supporting information, Table S1). However, this LOD level is still much lower than the LOD of 0.2 μg L−1 (∼0.014 nM) achieved by the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) using antibodies as the recognition element [55].

The selectivity of the MIP NPs for the target protein was further proved by comparing their FL response toward Lyz with that toward other reference proteins having different Mw, isoelectric point (pI) and number of exposed His residues under a same concentration (1.6 μM). The Mw, pI and number of exposed His residues of all the proteins studied are Lyz (Mw 14 k, pI 11.1, 1 His), cytochrome c (Cyt c, Mw 12.4 k, pI 10.2, 1 His), trypsin (Mw 24 k, pI 10.5, 1 His), HSA (Mw 66 k, pI 4.8, 2 His), bovine serum albumin (BSA, Mw 67 k, pI 4.8, 2 His), ovalbumin (OVA, Mw 44.5 k, pI 4.5, 1 His) [56]. As seen from Fig. 4c, the (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP for the reference proteins was within about 1.0–1.3, significantly lower than 5.8 for Lyz, because that Lyz could preferentially entered the Lyz-imprinted cavities to lighten the neighboring CDs. Furthermore, the FL intensity of the MIP NPs toward Lyz (1.6 μM) was almost not affected by mixing with a competitive protein, Cyt c (up to five-fold higher concentration) with the similar characters including Mw, pI and the number of exposed His residues (Fig. 4d).

The possible applicability of the MIP NPs as FL sensors was further studied in diluted chicken egg-white samples spiked with different Lyz concentrations. Satisfactory recoveries and accuracies were attained with a recovery range of 97.9–101.4% and RSDs of 1.1–3.5% for the spiked samples with three concentrations (Table 1 ). Therefore, the MIP sensors may be practically employed for sensitive and accurate determination of low Lyz contents in real samples.

The MIP NPs after binding of Lyz for FL measurements were regenerated by the same way as the template was removed during the synthesis of the imprinted particles, and then reused for the FL analysis. The FL response of the MIP NPs to Lyz remained almost intact after up to four use/regeneration cycles (supporting information, Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary extension to imprinting of HSA
ce:section.ce:para: For proof of the versatility of the present imprinting strategy for FL sensing of other His-exposed proteins, we further investigated the imprinting of HSA with 2 exposed His residues and significantly larger Mw than Lyz (66 k vs 14 k). The HSA-imprinted and non-imprinted NPs (denoted as MIP-HSA and NIP-HSA, respectively) were synthesized in the similar way employed for Lyz imprinting with the same template and nanosupport concentrations and monomer molar ratio, but the total monomer concentration was increased to 0.5% and the molar ratio of GMA-IDA-Cu2+/HSA increased to 4/1. The HSA-imprinted NPs also showed FL enhancement on binding of the target (supporting information, Fig. S10A), as the Lyz-imprinted NPs demonstrated above. It was also verified that utilization of Cu2+ mediated coordination for the construction of HSA-imprinted NPs could remarkably enhance their specific FL response to the target protein (supporting information, Fig. S10B). The MIP- HSA NPs showed satisfactory FL response selectivity toward HSA(supporting information, Figs. S10C and S10D). It is worth noting that, even though the homology of the HSA and BSA sequence is approximately 80% [57], the selective recognition of HSA against BSA was still successfully achieved, indicative of the generation of high-quality HSA-imprinted cavities.

In summary, with Lyz as well as HSA as His-exposed protein templates, we have demonstrated an effective method for the synthesis of FL protein-imprinted NPs by aqueous precipitation copolymerization of Cu2+ chelating monomer with an OEG monomer over CDs-anchored SiO2 NPs for enhanced protein detection. The imprinted cavities formed by utilization of the metal coordination could significantly increase the specific FL response to the target protein. The as-synthesized protein-imprinted NPs showed satisfactory FL sensing performance in terms of IFSV, LOD, selectivity, and also recoveries and accuracy for real samples. It may be a versatile approach for the construction of high-performance FL protein-imprinted NPs, considering that most proteins bear exposed His residues.

Zhiqiang Liu: Synthesized and characterized of properties of the materials, writing, review & editing. Shiting Zhang: Synthesized and characterized properties of the materials. Siyu Jin: Characterized properties of the materials. Xingjia Feng: Formal analysis, contributed to data interpretation. Yufei Bai: Formal analysis, contributed to data interpretation. Xiao Han: Formal analysis, contributed to data interpretation. Guoqi Fu: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21674051) and the National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFC11044001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.123070.
